10X Advisor Network: Welcome to our Bonus 10X Business Buying Case Study. We’re
here with Aaron Christensen, CEO of Christensen Automotive. We’ll be talking about
several acquisitions he’s made throughout the years. Including his largest acquisition
to date -- a multi-store automotive repair business acquisition. The acquired
locations were in a different state than Aaron’s core business. And he made the
acquisition with the help of our advisor, Brandon Dawson. We’ll cover what each deal
looked like… how they came about… hard lessons learned… and the rewards of
acquiring them. Welcome Aaron!
Aaron Christensen: Thanks for having me!
10X Advisor Network: Let’s start with your background for our students. Who are
you? What kind of business do you run? And just a very brief history of how you
started your business and your experiences with business acquisition.
Aaron Christensen: Sure. I'm the CEO of Christensen Automotive. It’s an automotive
repair business that my parents started in 1998. Basically, the business does
everything you would do in a dealership, but independent of a dealership.
After spending some time in the military, my brother and I came back and acquired
the business from our parents, after getting our feet wet.
So, when we started, we started with just two shops, and then, we kind of added
another shop, added another shop.
So then, we were sitting at four shops total. And then, that's kind of when we got
involved with Brandon Dawson and the Cardone Ventures network. And as we
continued to work with them, it was through 2020.
We got started with them at the beginning, right before COVID and all that stuff really
started affecting the way people do business and all that.
As we went through 2020, it was really nice to work with them because it was that
constant touch point of, okay, what are people doing?
We were getting introduced to a bunch of other great business owners and really
starting thinking about whether we were doing the right things.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 21 --

We wondered if we were taking the right steps as we navigated through 2020? We
ended up finishing 2020 about 10% up from 2019.
So really, just taking and capitalizing on the areas that we thought we could capitalize
on with the advice and guidance from Brandon and the Cardone Ventures team.
That transitioned to, towards the end of 2020, looking at acquiring businesses in a
new region. Kind of going out outside of our home base, if you will, and going into a
new area.
We were primarily in Northern Nevada. And then, we were looking at different
markets. And then, as the opportunity came up, we started talking with Brandon about
this opportunity.
We really started diving into what it was going to take to acquire this business. We
wondered what were the blind spots, if you will, that I didn't have as a business
owner, because I'd never done an acquisition of that size.
We leaned into our relationship with Brandon to figure out, what do we need to be
looking for? What are the hidden gotchas? What are the things to start to dive into, to
make sure that we're doing all of our due diligence appropriately?
Ultimately, we acquired that business in Las Vegas, which was another eight stores.
So effectively, we became a hundred percent bigger or 150% bigger than the previous
year thanks to working with Brandon and the team. They helped us make sure that all
of our steps are in place.
Because the systems need to be solid if you’re managing a business from a distance.
There are many more things that we needed to put together as an organization to
make sure that we were successful because I can't spend my time 450 miles apart
from each other.
10X Advisor Network: How did you find the business in Las Vegas to acquire?
Aaron Christensen: After we did what’s called our “platform review” we realized
"Now it's time we can expand. We have the capabilities to do it." So, what I did was I just
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 21 --

got on the internet and just started looking at different opportunities. And just started
making some phone calls.
This was at the end of 2020, but as things started to get traction, I was getting called
from different business brokers. There are multiple different ways to expand, but
before we expanded down to Las Vegas, people would approach us and say, "Hey, do
you want to buy? You guys have a great reputation. Do you guys want to buy our business?"
So we needed to ask, "Okay, well, what are you looking for? What’s the price? What do you
think that you're going to get for it?” Because obviously, we have to have a starting
point. This is before we were introduced to Brandon and Cardone Ventures.
When you're acquiring one operation, there's a lot less involved with it, when it's just
a single location. But the things that I learned after our big acquisition was, I can
reflect back on those single acquisitions going, "Okay, there was a lot of stuff that I
didn't know. I was just dealing with somebody who wasn't at the same level as I was."
And I wouldn't say I was an expert at anything, by any means. I just happened to be in
the right place, where preparation meets opportunities.
So, going into this big acquisition in Las Vegas, the things that we were looking at
were, “okay, where is it at? What's the location? Does it make sense for us to be there?
What are the demographics?”
And a lot of this stuff is stuff that we learned working with Brandon. So, as we started
diving into all that stuff, just figuring out: Does this make sense for our market? Does
this make sense for our business? Does this make sense for us, as an organization, to
expand to this area?
And then, getting into the negotiation process, we really leaned into Brandon. We’d
call him and ask all sorts of questions about what was possible.
Brandon always says the quality of your question will deliver the quality of your
results.
So, the better questions you can ask, the better answers you're going to get.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 21 --

So, instead of the traditional "Hey, how much do you want for your business? Okay.
Then let's talk from there" versus, "What does your customer look like?"
Because there are a lot of the things that we had to do, that we've done this last year
after the acquisition. For example, we're turning over our customer base because we
knew we were going to have to, because they were advertising to a different customer
than what we advertise to. We knew there was going to be a little bit of a lag as we
transitioned through those customers.
And how that Las Vegas acquisition came up was, I had called a broker after looking
on, I think it was, LoopNet or something like that.
I called a broker, and when I started talking to the broker, he had a couple listings of
multi-store operations, because I didn't want to go into Las Vegas with just a single
store.
I wanted to go into that market with multiple stores. And so, as the conversation
developed, we started looking at a couple different opportunities that they had. And
they were franchised and stuff like that. It wasn't going to work. It wasn't the right fit
for what we had decided we wanted. So it was very clear from the beginning on that
going, "Okay, no. Let's not waste our time on that."
So, knowing what you want is a big thing.
He had called me back probably a couple weeks later. He said, "Hey, I got an interesting
thing, on a deal I was working with prior, that everything kind of fell through." It was a
large acquisition that just fell apart towards the end.
He asked: "Would you be interested in this?" And I said, "Well, yeah. It doesn't hurt to have
a conversation."
At this point I went and talked to Brandon, and told him about the deal that we were
looking at, he said, "Okay, what about this? What about that? What about that?" So
now, it really opened up my eyes and expanded my knowledge base on, what
questions do I need to be asking the broker to ask the seller?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 21 --

It's hard, too, when you're dealing with brokers because it's like buying a house. You
want to move at a faster speed than everybody else wants to move. Or somebody else
might want to move at a faster speed than what you want to move.
And it's just being very cautious of, if the seller's trying to get done with the deal, then
why are they trying to get done with that deal so quickly? Versus why are we trying to
buy so quickly? And so on and so forth.
You need to go through a little bit of a dance where you make sure everybody's in the
right frame of mind when you're asking questions.
But we had some conversations with Brandon after we started figuring out what this
deal looked like, and then started creating some of the parameters we were going to
aim for.
For example, what's the end result of what we're looking to do with this deal?
10X Advisor Network: What was your arrangement with the business broker?
Aaron Christensen: This is where asking good questions of your broker comes into
play. For example, asking them, “How do you fit into this opportunity?” It worked out
for us, because it wasn't really any cash out of pocket to the broker for us, because he
was also the listing broker.
And that's kind of how the deal came up, because it was a “pocket listing” that he had.
[10X Advisor Network Note: A “pocket listing” is a business listing that the broker is
keeping private or “in their pocket” instead of distributing it publicly through a
listing.]
But the broker was very involved. I still talk to him to this day. He calls and checks in
with me, asks me when I'm ready to move to other markets. And he still wants to work
with us.
Will we work with them down the road? Who knows, we'll see what that looks like. But
it's just realizing everything's negotiable too. And so if a broker goes in and they say,
these are the parameters, then that's just realizing that's a starting point.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 21 --

And you look at Brandon Dawson… or you look at Grant Cardone, and you look at these
guys that have tons and tons of deals, big, big deals under their belt, they could
probably go in and talk about every single deal they've done.
And you’ll probably see there were different parameters behind every deal.
So, you need to be creative and ask questions of the seller like, “Would you be
comfortable with this? Would you be comfortable with that?”
As we continued through our deal, the structure of the deal probably changed three or
four times before it was all final said and done.
Don’t be afraid to ask those questions of what the seller is comfortable with.
And then, also know what your parameters are as a business owner, or a potential
business owner. And if the opportunity doesn't work for you, then you have got to cut
it. Don't get emotionally invested.
Because when you get emotionally invested, that's when those sharks will come out
and they'll take advantage of you. So, if a seller is not meeting what parameters you're
looking for in a particular deal, don't be afraid to cut it off and then walk away from it,
because there are going to be other deals out there.
10X Advisor Network: Did you have a specific deal structure in mind when you were
looking for the acquisition?
Aaron Christensen: I was really open to anything as long as it made sense. Our Las
Vegas acquisition was different than the previous smaller acquisitions that we've
made.
If the seller's able to finance, that's always a great thing. Because that way you don’t
have to deal with the bank and all that stuff.
So if a seller's able to finance, awesome.
This particular one, the seller did finance on this big Las Vegas acquisition.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 21 --

And then we'd also put some money down on the thing too.
And then it's always looking at the next thing for the deal too. So looking at, okay,
seller finance at first, then I'm looking at two years into the deal to go, take that, the
remaining note, and refinance it with a bank.
Because after that time, we can show the bank that we're capable of making money.
This is the same thing you hear Grant talking about when he’s financing his real estate
deals. Five to 10 years down the road, he'll go refinance the thing with the bank, and
then extend it out for another X amount of years. And then, boom, now the cash flow
is really starting to pile in on that acquisition.
And it's the same thing with business acquisitions. It’s making sure the business can
cash flow the note. And then not being afraid to not take money the first year or two,
to really pump up the business, so when you do go to refi, now you have a bunch of
cash flowing in, and then you go into the next cycle of expansion.
10X Advisor Network: What was important to the seller? What were they like
throughout the process?
Aaron Christensen: Looking at the Las Vegas acquisition, which is the biggest one
I’ve done, the seller probably could have continued on running the business. But he
was definitely burnt out. And so that's part of asking those right questions, of what's
their motivation in having the conversation to sell their business?
So burnout was definitely a factor for him. His heart wasn't in it anymore. And so he
was just like, now is probably a good time -- if I have a qualified buyer -- to walk
away from this thing.
And some of the other smaller acquisitions we've done were with the family legacy
type of seller. The guy was very concerned about his customers. And he had started the
business from ground zero, built the building, all that stuff.
And there was a lot of conversation around, him wanting to make sure we would take
care of his customers. You have to be able to hear that and then talk about that in the
deal making process. You need to reassure him you’re going to take care of his
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 21 --

customers.” I remember telling him, “That's why you approached us, because you like
our business model,” so on and so forth.
For the very first acquisition we did, it was a distressed business and they needed to
get out of it. So when you're dealing with distressed businesses,you can operate with
great speed and you can go in and take over for probably a really good price. But
realize that there's going to be a lot more work involved too.
With the businesses we acquired in Las Vegas, with all those businesses, we changed
the names right away. We were transparent about that from the beginning with the
seller. And the seller wasn’t too terribly attached. I know some I've heard some stories
where the people were really attached to the business’s name.
The name wouldn't be necessarily a deal breaker for me because after you acquire the
business, you can do whatever you want with it. It's your business. So just be able to
think outside the box on that.
With the big acquisition we did in Las Vegas, there was a little bit of distress too. So we
dealt with a lot of employee turnover, which I think was a learning point for us.
So having that in the back of your mind, to be able to identify the things that, when
you take over a large organization is, being able to make decisions quickly to get those
old employees out that probably aren't a good fit for what you want to do with the new
business.
10X Advisor Network: What are the differences between the types of questions you
have to ask of a distressed business seller and a seller with a business in great shape?
Aaron Christensen: You have to understand the motives of the seller in each
situation.
We talked about it a little bit at the beginning about understanding or asking those
quality questions of, flat out, why do you want to sell your business? And why do you
think that we would be a good fit to buy your business?
Versus somebody who could continue to run the business if they wanted to.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 21 --

They're going to be the ones that drive by post-acquisition all the time to see how it's
doing.That will slowly stop over time, but it starts that way.
The one small business that we bought where the guy was really concerned about his
customers, for example, he kept coming back.
And he was a customer of ours, and paid the price for our service and all that stuff. And
was a great customer.
Very positive, great promoter for us, all that stuff.
Versus a distressed business, they needed to get out of there so fast, because people
were beating down their door for money. So that process is a little bit more of a colder
transaction if you will. “This is what we're going to give you. You guys need it more than
we need it.”
Then you need to be able to identify that early in the transaction is key to how you're
going to move through the rest of the transaction. For example, with our Las Vegas
acquisition, the seller was kind of involved with the customers and stuff like that.
But he was kind of an absentee owner. So if he drives by the locations, great, I don't
care. It doesn't bother me.
But I've had a couple conversations with him after the acquisition. And he still talks
very fondly of the business, and so on and so forth. He wants to know how it's going
and all that.
And at that point, it doesn't matter, because we’ve fully taken over.
But it's no different than selling a product. It's finding out what the motive is for
somebody to come in and buy that product.
It's the same thing when you're going through and buying a business.
What's the motivation for that person to sell you their business? You should be asking
that question.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 21 --

10X Advisor Network: Could you recap at a very high level the steps it took to
acquire the multi-store Las Vegas acquisition?
Aaron Christensen: Yes, so one of the big steps going through to acquire a business
is, number one they're going to want an NDA. Everybody's going to want a fully
executed NDA from all parties. So reaching out to brokers, or if somebody comes and
talks to you, it's always nice to sign those NDAs just to have that on file so a lot of
people aren't talking about the deal.
Then with a broker, signing an NDA, helps you figure out where the business is at and
it allows you to get to a starting point about price?
Then, the next thing is the letter of intent. So the intention of, “hey, we want to buy
your business,” because that's going to set up due diligence.
When you sign a letter of intent, make sure that your letter of intent is written well for
you buying the business.
So then that way, when you go to do your due diligence, if the thing starts looking like
not a good business to buy, that's when you can walk away from everything and it
doesn't cost you any money.
So after the letter of intent, you do your due diligence, getting the lawyers involved to
go through the items. For example, are you assuming a lease? Are you buying a
building? What are all the different things buried in there?
If you're taking over a lease, it’s understanding what's in that lease. How does the
lease look? How long is left on the lease? What are the terms of the lease?
For us, being in the automotive repair business, we were concerned with “what does
the equipment look like? Is it all operational?”
Basically you need to find out if what they're telling you is true and accurate?
That means looking at all their financial data, looking at their customer lists, looking
at what kind of stuff they've been selling, etc.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 21 --

So that's probably the longest piece of really diving in and figuring out, are there any
red flags that are going to stop this deal from going through?
And then moving from that is kind of figuring out, what's the closing date?
Do we need to extend it out or anything like that?
And then after that, after everybody signs off on the due diligence piece, then it's time
to move to close.
And after you close, it's time to take over and start operating the business.
10X Advisor Network: Who did you surround yourself with for these acquisitions?
Did you just use all the same accountants, lawyers that you would in the course of
doing your normal business? Or did you look for someone specific to either the area,
or for that size acquisition?
Aaron Christensen: This goes back to asking the right questions. So as we went
through this process we prioritized asking the right questions to the people that we
were working with.
So we worked with our same accountant that we've been working with for years.
And then we had recently just started working with a business lawyer.
Before we started this deal, I asked him, “What's the largest deal you've ever done?” And
so then he told me what the largest deal he had done was and it was a little bit bigger
than the deal that we were working on. So I was like, okay, cool. You have the
expertise. You've done something of this size before.
The one thing you don't want to do is to have your professional advisors -- your
accountant or your lawyer, learning new things through your acquisition process as
you're learning new things.
So make sure that your support elements are capable of doing something that you're
trying to do for the first time. So for me, I reached out to Brandon Dawson because
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 21 --

Brandon has done large acquisitions like this before. He's done large deals. So he was
my expertise coming in as the business owner.
Our lawyer had done a deal that was a little bit bigger. So he had the expertise to do
this.
Our accountant had done deals like this.
So we had everybody in our corner, if you will. And that made us capable of moving
with that attack speed to get this deal done and make sure that there's no red flags.
You need to have that expertise to help you move forward, because especially if
somebody's never acquired a business before, there's a lot of unknowns. So it's just
finding the right people, finding the support elements to help you move through the
acquisition process as quickly as possible and with confidence.
10X Advisor Network: You mentioned employee turnover. What lessons did you learn
about managing employees in the business you acquired?
Aaron Christensen: This is a great question. I would definitely do things differently
next time. I would plus up my staff, train my key employees and then when we
acquired the business, I would dispatch them out to the new acquisition, to run the
new locations. In our line of work, with technicians and stuff like that in the back, I
would probably keep them in place just to kind of continue the workflow.
But for the guys on the counter, our managers and roles like that in the stores, I would
definitely have somebody trained before we acquired it. And then that way when we
did acquire, they would go in and be full speed ahead with how I do business versus
trying to train somebody on the job from how they used to do business to how we do
business. That was a huge, huge gap. And it killed momentum for us after we took
over. It took probably about six months to cycle through all those employees. On the
Las Vegas acquisition our employee turnover rate was probably about 90%.
There are not a lot of employees from before the acquisition that work for us still.
There's a few, but most we either got rid of or they self-selected out when they
realized that they weren't going to be able to keep up with how we do business.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 21 --

10X Advisor Network: Did you do any creative financing that leveraged assets in the
business you acquired?
Aaron Christensen: This last acquisition taught me a lot about just being creative
with financing and asking those questions. I know I was talking about it a little bit
before, but being creative to ask those questions of like, “hey, would you guys do this?
Would you do that? The seller financing, what are the terms of the seller financing? What
are they comfortable with?”
And then because it's all, you're signing legally bound agreements to make sure that
we got to cover whatever that note is. We have to cover that note. But being able to
understand that, okay, if I can go and I can sustain this note for two years, then I can
go refinance with the Small Business Administration after that because now I have
two years solid of books.
Make sure that you have everything in place and then go to a bank and do the
traditional SBA route or start looking around before you even go in and you can do a
bank finance and stuff like that to make sure. I probably would have that conversation
with the bank going before you even got to the due diligence and that’s because banks
tend to move slow. They're going to want to see a lot of information like the SBA, if
anybody's ever done a deal with the SBA, it's like dragging nails all across the
chalkboard.
That’s big government versus you as an entrepreneur. Governments move slow and
entrepreneurs move fast. So with the finance, it's whatever you really feel comfortable
with as a business owner or potential business owner. You need to think about what
strategy would make you feel comfortable. Are you able to make it work? Are you to
look past the bigger picture of that traditional financing model? Do you have a
network that could finance it privately? We’ve done that kind of stuff in the past.
10X Advisor Network: Who made up your deal team and who was your lead advisor?
Aaron Christensen: Having a lead advisor for you, especially if you've never
acquired a business before, is huge. As I worked with Brandon on this Las Vegas
acquisition it was such a huge relief because I knew he was capable because of his
experience and expertise. So, he was helping me ask questions that made me a lot
more confident, especially when I started asking questions to the broker, because they
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 21 --

were thinking “holy shit, this guy knows what he's talking about.” All of those questions
came from Brandon.
So now I'm learning as the process goes on. So I know as we went through the whole
deal stage is making sure I'm asking the right questions at the right time to the right
people. And then if something came up, I can turn back and lean into Brandon and tell
him what's happening. And I'd get a response instantaneously because he knew
exactly what to do.
He was already three steps ahead of where I was, because he already knew what
questions I was going to ask or roughly about what I was going to ask because he had
done this before. He's bought and sold businesses so he understands both sides of the
deal. He understands what the seller's going through. He understands where the
brokers are at. He understands where we're at as the buyer.
And so being able to really lean into him as lead advisor was huge for us. There was
definitely questions I wouldn't have known.
Brandon says it all all the time, he goes, you don't know what you don't know. And
there was definitely a lot of stuff I didn't know. I knew or I didn't even know that I
didn't know.
He would help me ID I didn't know something so that I could learn about it. It wasn’t
like he was going to sit there and give me all the answers. He would prompt me with
questions, like “'hey, what about this?” And then I’d go and do my research.
Because the way we all learn, and grow is to do the research ourselves.
But being able to expose me to those different things through the deal process was
huge. If anyone watching this is going to lean into Brandon or Cardone Ventures,
they're a great, great, great resource.
The size of the deals that they've done are probably going to be comparable to what
anybody's doing at any given time. And their deal experience would probably match a
close advisor or mentor that you have that’s gone through deals like that.
When you’re getting your deal team, remember, it’s always asking that question:
“What's the biggest deal you've ever done?”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 21 --

And if the deal you're doing is bigger than what they've ever done, you probably want
to go find somebody who's done a bigger deal because they're going to be able to
understand what you're going through.
Don’t be afraid to ask those questions – to your lead advisor, to your lawyer, you’re
your accountant, even the business broker. That’s the only way to make sure that
everybody involved is qualified to push the deal through.
10X Advisor Network: Now that you've done so many acquisitions, how are you
thinking about scaling it and acquiring other businesses in the future?
Aaron Christensen: Because we now have two separate regions after acquiring these
businesses, our priority right now is all about making sure our product is the same
across the board and making sure everybody's speaking the same language. That’s all
on me as a business owner to make sure everybody in my organization is doing it.
If you have one person that's talking in a different way than the other person, you
have to get that fixed before you go and expand again, because if you have problems in
one area, you're going to have in all areas. And so, this is one area where we've had a
trial by fire over the last year.
I won't expand again until we are a hundred percent with that. You think of a
McDonald's. When you walk into a McDonald's it's the same whether you're in South
America, Europe or Texas or Washington state or wherever. It's the same product.
And so as we grow and scale into different areas, I can't be everywhere to make sure
that they're doing the same thing. So I need to have those checks and balances in place
to make sure that my teams are operating the way that I would operate the business.
Brandon talks a lot about “model, mimic master.” And that’s what we’re focusing on
to make sure that the people under my regional managers are doing exactly what I
want them to do. I’m constantly checking in with the store managers and making sure
that they're constantly checking in with the employees underneath them.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 21 --

That outcome starts with the top. And if I have a location that's missing one thing and
they're not doing it right, then I must expect that all the other 12 locations are doing
the same thing.
Brandon’s taught me that where there's confusion, there's failure because
everybody's not shooting towards the same target. If you're blindly shooting out
there, then it will be harder for you to grow and scale. He also talks about the break
points. We went through massive break points this year. I think we went through, it
was two or three different break points this year. It was painful. It was hard. We're still
dealing with it but it's that constant grind and learning from the mistakes that we've
made to not repeat those same mistakes.
And before we even think about going into another region, I want to make sure that
everything that I have right now is a hundred percent operational, productive, making
money. When that gets to that and it stays that way for probably about 12 months,
then that's when I’ll think things are going really well. Then that's when I'll start
going out and start looking at a new region or acquiring more businesses in Las Vegas
or Northern Nevada.
It's kind of staying where we're at and growing where we're at or go find that third
region to expand our footprint. But if we're not doing it right we’re not going to rush
to expand. A lot of our managers that were in our Northern Nevada locations, they felt
that operational pain this year. They felt the pain of growth. I had a lot of times that
they asked me in the summertime, they're like, when does this stop?
And I said, it never stops. If we're going to continue to grow, we’re going to have to
continue to go past those pain points. And that's that stretching those rubber bands
and making sure that one, we're not breaking them, but two is making sure we're
stretching them to full capacity because then they're going to get used to the new
pace.
They're going to get used to the new way, making sure that we're operating our
business the way we need to be operating it, to take care of our customers. That way
we have a flow and then customer retention.
We’re working to have everything documented on paper. That way, when we go to
open up a new region, I have an operating manual for how we do things. “This is how
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 21 --

we answer the phones… this is how we provide our services, etc.” That way
everything's exactly the same across the board. And that's that scaling piece that we
get excited about. And sometimes people forget about that work because they’re
looking at all the opportunity.
That's when you need to get back down to basics of how do we get to the opportunity
to grow, or how does an entrepreneur get the opportunity to make their own business?
Because they're going, I can do this better than the place I'm working at. I can do this
better than the guy I'm working for. Or for me is, we can do automotive repair better
than anybody else in the country. But if we're actually not doing it, then it doesn't
make any sense to grow and scale because the house of cards will end up falling down
as time goes.
10X Advisor Network: 	How do you think your businesses would be right now if you
had expanded by opening new shops yourself instead of buying an existing business?
Aaron Christensen: Well, the nice part about acquiring is you already have
customers coming in. Whether they're your type of customers you want to promote to
or not. But you have revenue coming in from basically day one versus a startup. When
I got out of the military and I started working for my dad, we opened up a shop as a
startup. And I went and ran that shop. And I was like, I don't think I'd ever do this
again. It's too slow for the industry that we're in. If you have a unique product or
something like that, then it might make sense to be a startup. But for us in the
automotive repair business, it's if I can go and tap into that customer base. And then
over the first six months sift through that customer base and filter out the customers I
don't want or convert the customers from the people they were promoting to before
versus to what we want. It makes the business grow that much faster.
One of the single businesses we acquired before we did our Las Vegas expansion did
$250,000 in sales their last year. That next year that we operated, we did $930,000. So
it was taking what they were missing and really expanding on that.
Acquisitions aren’t perfect. The Las Vegas acquisition, again, taught us a lot of lessons
learned. Because when we acquired the single location prior to Las Vegas, that's when
I put my team in there. So when we expanded to Las Vegas, I didn't have a team to put
in because it was so large.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 21 --

So those where the pain points. That's where the lessons learned came from, of had I
had my own team to put into those places, then I think the attack speed would've been
a lot quicker this year.
But those are helpful lessons to learn. If you don't go through these lessons, then
you're bound to make a bigger mistake sooner or later. Having learned that now, I can
have my own team go in a place for the next acquisition.
10X Advisor Network: What’s the #1 thing outside of the lessons you’ve shared that
someone acquiring a business for the first time should know?
Aaron Christensen: The thing I would say for somebody going out to acquire a
business or expand massively on the business that they currently have is to make sure
you know what your goals and dreams are and then don't let anybody derail you from
that. Being able to push through the adversity is important because you're going to get
a lot of feedback, especially if you acquire a large business that has a lot of people that
you're going to acquire.
You're going to get a lot of pushback on how you want to do business, but it's that
grind of being the business owner and knowing what you want and how you want your
business ran and stick to it. So, have that plan.
I have my on my desk here, I have my business platform and I review that thing all the
time. And that's the thing that I look at to remind myself I'm doing it right. And what
everybody else is saying, doesn't matter. It's filter out all the noise, all the bullshit and
stick to what your plan is. And continue to grind because the people that aren't going
to be aligned with you are going to identify themselves really early. And don't be
afraid to get rid of them. Get them out and get somebody in that aligns with what you
want to do with your business.
You might also have feedback coming from really close friends and family and stuff
like that. But just remember you don't have to convince anybody of what your goals
and dreams are. Because the hardest people to convince are close friends and family
going, “why are you putting in so much time to your business? Why are you doing
this? Why are you doing that? Spend more time with the family.”
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 21 --

You hear about Grant talking about this all the time. If you're going to give up on your
goals or your family's not going to support you and your goals or close friends, then
you know it's time to really evaluate. Take those hard looks at yourself to go, am I
surrounding myself with the right people?
That's why I surround myself with Brandon and Grant. When I get down and I go... I'll
go hop on YouTube and I'll look up Grant Cardone videos and I'll watch just to listen to
what he says.
Because you listen to him and he's always grinding, but he has a great support
network around him. And everybody understands what his dreams are. Everybody
around him that's really close to him, understands that where he's going to. And then
they're all behind him pushing him. You listen to Elena talk to talk about Grant at
Growth Con. She is pushing him harder than he's pushing himself sometimes. But it's
you've got to be able to surround yourself with people that are going to help you
support your dreams.
They’ll help push you when you get down because things aren't going exactly the way
you thought they were going. Then stand back up and go get after it. If you're not
getting after it, then what are you doing?
And that's really entrepreneurship. That's growing and scaling. It’s not being afraid.
And then when you do get down, look to those people that have done bigger things
than you and understand that there's a lot of time left to work through these pain
points and understand that there’s a lesson to be learned.
Learn from your pain points. Learn from the mistakes that you make. Lean into the
people that have done it before. Lean into the people that believe in you. If you go ask
Grant or Brandon what we're doing and you ask them, do you believe in what he's
doing? I would assume that they're going to say, “Absolutely, yeah. But he's not doing
it fast enough. He's not doing it big enough. His dreams aren't big enough.”
That's the kind of stuff that they're going to tell me. He needs to think bigger. He
needs to think quicker. The attack speed needs to be quicker.
Stop thinking about what you're doing and just go do it. And those are the people that
I surround myself with. And those are the people that I look at on a constant basis to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 21 --

go, okay, am I doing the right thing? The people really close to me are saying the same
thing. They're going, go out there and get it done. You can do it. And so, it's cut out the
negative noise and get with the people that believe in you 100%.
10X Advisor Network: What are your exit plans?
Aaron Christensen: We have specific growth points that we want to be at in 10 years.
And you know, you look at this industry as a whole and you look at all the things that
are going on outside. Like Elon Musk is pushing electric vehicles. And you've got Ford,
GM, the big auto manufacturers are going to electric vehicles, stuff like that. An
electric vehicle has a 10th of the parts on it as a traditional internal gas combustion
vehicle has. So the maintenance aspect of those vehicles, it's going to be a lot less than
what we have on the road now. So you look at pushing the next 10 years as taking
much cash and reinvesting that money into other things that are going to make more
money.
So you look at things like Cardone Capital on the real estate investment side. Grant
and Brandon have a lot of great things coming out. Grant has a lot of great things
coming out on the business side. And so looking at different avenues to take the
money that we acquire now to make that generational type wealth.
And then, as we get to a certain dollar level is, if somebody comes across that is going
to write me a check big enough one day, absolutely. Everything's for sale. But, is it the
right time for me? I'm 38 years old. I'm young. I've got a lot of time left. So, in 10 years
I'm 48, cash out then. Reinvest the money into something else that's going to put the
money to work for me.
And so really it's kind of just pushed towards that next 10 years. And then after that is,
see how big our business is to go out and sell at that point. Or just continue to grow it
and then just run the life cycle of the business. And as we put money in other things,
and invest in other things, then after that, just start closing down as electric vehicles
take over or hydrogen vehicles take over or whatever the case is.
But I think we have a long time before you start seeing these gas cars them off the
road. We get a lot of cars that are 10 years old still, and people are hanging onto their
cars because trying to buy a new car these days is outrageous. And the price of
everything is just going to continue to go up. And so people are just going to hang onto
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 21 --

what they have and maintain what they have. And that's a great position for us to be
in.
